slides.title: Text in Python = Strings
slides.body: Almost every program handles text — names, messages, commands. In Python, any piece of text is called a **string**, and it's one of the first superpowers you'll reach for.
slides.tip: Strings are everywhere: user input, file names, printed output — all strings.

slides.title: Create Strings with Quotes
slides.body: Wrap text in `'single'`, `"double"`, or `"""triple"""` quotes — all three make a string. Mix styles to include apostrophes or quotes inside your text without trouble.
slides.code:
greeting = 'Hello, world!'
message = "It's a great day!"
print(greeting)
print(message)
slides.output:
Hello, world!
It's a great day!

slides.title: Join and Repeat Strings
slides.body: Use `+` to **concatenate** (glue) strings together, and `*` to repeat one. To mix a number into a string, wrap it with `str()` first — Python won't do it for you.
slides.code:
name = "Ada"
print("Hello, " + name + "!")
print("-" * 20)
print("Score: " + str(99))
slides.tip: Forgetting str() when concatenating a number is the #1 beginner TypeError.

slides.title: Measure Length with len()
slides.body: `len()` counts every character in a string — letters, spaces, punctuation, all of them. It returns a plain integer you can use right away.

slides.title: Strings Don't Change — They Rebuild
slides.body: Strings are **immutable**: you can't alter a character in place. Instead, build a brand-new string and assign it to the variable. Escape sequences like `\n` (newline) and `\t` (tab) let you embed special characters too.
slides.tip: You now know creation, quotes, concatenation, len(), and immutability — the full string starter kit!
